about.title: About
about.section0_1: Onepiece Random Defense (OPRD) is not a standalone commercial game. Instead, it was born as a <bold>custom map</bold> derived from Blizzard Entertainment's classic real-time strategy (RTS) game, <bold>Warcraft III: The Frozen Throne</bold>.
about.section0_2: Since its release in 2002, Warcraft III has offered epic campaign stories and an innovative hero leveling system. However, its greatest legacy is undoubtedly the incredibly powerful, bundled <bold>"World Editor."</bold> This highly open editor allows players to utilize built-in triggers, the JASS scripting language, and custom 3D models and sound effects. It completely shatters the original framework of RTS games, paving the way for entirely new genres—including Tower Defense (TD), Role-Playing Games (RPG), and Aeon of Strife (AOS) hero battle maps.
about.section0_3_title: Why did Warcraft III become the cradle for this game?
about.section0_3:
• Freely Import External Resources: Developers can directly import 3D models and sound effects of anime characters into the map.
  • Ultimate Item and Unit Mechanics: Warcraft III's built-in inventory and synthesis logic provide the perfect execution environment for 'random draw and fusion' gameplay.
  • Nurturing Countless Classics: Just as this editor once gave birth to the globally popular 'DotA' map—the predecessor of Dota 2 and League of Legends—its open community ecosystem has allowed highly sentimental fan works like 'Onepiece Random Defense' to sustain their legendary life for over a decade.
about.section1_lead: The core map logic of "Onepiece Random Defense" is built upon the dual pressure of <bold>"Line Defense"</bold> and <bold>"Story Progress."</bold> Unlike traditional tower defense games where you simply build towers to defend, this game requires players to invest excess military power and resources into the <bold>"Story Area"</bold> to destroy story island structures with extremely high HP, all while facing high-intensity waves of mobs.
about.section1_card1_body: Monsters constantly flood into a player's personal area. When the monster count in their area reaches a certain limit (usually $75$ or $80$ depending on the difficulty), the player will be <bold>instantly defeated</bold>. This requires the team to have sufficient "Slow" and "Stun" crowd control.
about.section1_card2_body: Story buildings must be destroyed before specific limit rounds. Destroying these buildings rewards all players with a large amount of <bold>Wood</bold>, <bold>Gold</bold>, and specific <bold>low-to-mid tier hero selection boxes</bold>. Players who do not progress the story will face complete resource exhaustion in the mid-game.
about.section1_save_body: ORDR features a mature Save Code mechanism using the system registry. Each time players clear the game on high difficulties (e.g., God or Hell), the system automatically exports an encrypted file to the Warcraft directory, recording their <bold>clear counts</bold> and accumulated points. The number of clears plays a crucial role in the game:
about.section1_save_list:
• Unlocking special titles and limited skins.
  • Increasing the reward multiplier for initial random Wisps or Gold.
about.section2_lead: In Random Defense, resources can be simplified into three tiers: <bold>Wisps</bold>, <bold>Wood</bold>, and <bold>Tokens</bold>
about.section2_card1_body: The basic production power sent by the system every round. It can be directly used to draw "Common" units, or serve as an essential base material when crafting top-tier heroes.
about.section2_card2_body: Used for drawing characters, crafting, or upgrading top-tier heroes. Wood is typically obtained by destroying story buildings, exchanging Wisps, or defeating bosses.
about.section3_lead: The <bold>"community coups," "hacking incidents," and "copyright wars"</bold> that have occurred over the ten-plus years of this game's history. Here is the most representative history compiled from Namu Wiki:
about.section3_acc1_title: 믘핰 (Mük-Hak)'s Sudden Departure and Forum Sale
about.section3_acc1_p1: In 2010, the original creator <bold>Mük-Hak</bold> successfully ported this project from StarCraft to Warcraft III, developing it all the way to <bold>version 3.1</bold> where the basic system was established. However, at this point, he suddenly lost interest in updating the map. Without notifying the community, he privately sold the Naver Official Forum (Café) directly to third-party advertisers and completely went offline.
about.section3_acc1_p2: At that time, the map code still had a strong Map Protector, and the community faced the crisis of discontinued updates. MORD guild officer siroyacha- relentlessly emailed the original author for a year, finally convincing him to share the unencrypted source map. It was then handed over to the new producer <bold>hantory</bold>, and the map was reborn.
about.section3_acc2_title: The Power Struggle and the "2,000 Members Mass Kick" Tragedy
about.section3_acc2_p1: After hantory took over, severe internal conflicts broke out due to major disagreements with the alliance of the three major official guilds (KORD, MORD, TORD) regarding the update direction. For a time, chat channels were filled with personal attacks and threats of real-life fights (현피).
about.section3_acc2_p2: In the chaos, MORD guild veteran kimms- attempted to split the forum after failing to demand the source code. Although he was banned by the vice-moderator, that same night, HaruK, a forum admin who supported him, used his privileges to <bold>maliciously kick out over 2,000 core players from the forum</bold> in an attempt to destroy the community. Although this incident was resolved later, the devastating struggle forced the exhausted producer to step down, ushering in a brand new era.
about.section3_acc3_title: "I'm going to clash with you today!" - The Eyab and Evemon Dispute
about.section3_acc3_p1: During the golden era, producer <bold>이얍 (Eyab)</bold> and vice-moderator <bold>Evemon</bold> grew the forum into a massive hub of over 390,000 members. However, irreconcilable conflicts arose between them regarding forum management policies: Evemon advocated for strict, militaristic moderation, while Eyab wanted a casual, harmonious community.
about.section3_acc3_p2: During a management argument in March 2015, Evemon strictly demanded that all posts follow a rigid format. Eyab stepped in to stop it, and Evemon retorted in the group: <bold>"I'm going to clash with you today."</bold> This was the final exchange between the legendary developer duo. Eyab revoked Evemon's administrator privileges, and they parted ways. Although they eventually reconciled via the gifting of the "Sabo Transcendence Wing" cosmetic, it remains a famous meme enjoyed by veteran players.
about.section3_acc4_title: Rebirth (R-version) Mutiny Controversy and Copyright Storm
about.section3_acc4_p1: In October 2019, community dissatisfaction peaked with the poorly received 8.9b update and Eyab's delays. The Naruto Defense team, without official authorization, reverse-engineered the map and released a modded version named <bold>Rebirth (R-version)</bold>, introducing major balance improvements and new features. This sparked a fierce debate on the official forum between advocates of progress and defenders of original orthodoxy.
about.section3_acc4_p2: In the middle of this internal strife, <bold>Daewon Media</bold> officially issued a strong Intellectual Property (IP) infringement warning. Under legal pressure, Naver closed the map distribution links. The original ORD was forced to permanently stop updating at <bold>version 11.089</bold>. However, the R-version team restructured the map, rewrote vast amounts of Warcraft scripts (JASS code), and ultimately revived ORDR as an independent open-source community on Discord and DC Inside, continuing updates to this day.
about.section4_sub_title: Warcraft Base Damage Mechanics & Armor Reduction Thresholds
about.section4_text1: Why is armor reduction so crucial for physical damage builds? It is directly related to Warcraft III's armor formulas.
about.section4_text2: For positive armor value \(A\), the Damage Reduction (DR) formula is:
about.section4_text3: However, when armor is reduced to a negative value \(A < 0\), the Damage Bonus is calculated as:
about.section4_text4: The optimal efficiency point is when armor is reduced to around \( -5 \). At \(-20\), the enemy takes \(71 \%\) extra damage (scaling slows down past this point). Compared to the \(60\%\) damage reduction at \(+25\) armor, the difference is massive. This is why physical damage builds must construct multiple armor-reduction aura characters.

footer.brand: ORD One Piece Random Defense (ORDR) Helper
footer.desc: This website is based on publicly available information from Namu Wiki/ordsearch.net, and the content is intended for academic research, player guides, and community promotion only.
footer.feedback: If you have any questions or find any incorrect information, please contact us.

translations.thirdPartyTutorial_h1: To use the auto-update owned characters feature, please install and enable the third-party tool first.
translations.thirdPartyTutorial_l2_1: Download the TMO proxy tool (includes instructions)
translations.thirdPartyTutorial_l2_2: (If using this feature on mobile, you need to configure the local network)
translations.thirdPartyTutorial_l3: Open the TMOGG tool and the proxy tool
translations.thirdPartyTutorial_l5: Enable the switch for this feature, you must allow the website permission the first time, 'connect success' will be displayed upon success
translations.thirdPartyTutorial_l6_1: Allow the website's [Apps on your device] permission. If not allowed, it will fail, and you need to manually enable the permission (on mobile, also grant 'Local Network' permission)
translations.thirdPartyTutorial_l6_2: (Click the 'lock' icon on the left side of the URL. In the pop-up menu, click 'Site Settings' and enable the 'Apps on your device' or 'Local Network' permission. After that, refresh the page to apply the changes.)
